title: ACCORD
subtitle: Intensive Glucose Lowering in Type 2 Diabetes — Summary, Why/How, MCQ & T/F Module

trialDesign.fullName: Action to Control Cardiovascular Risk in Diabetes (ACCORD) — Glycemia Trial
trialDesign.design: Multicenter randomized controlled trial with double 2×2 factorial BP and lipid substudies
trialDesign.population: 10,251 patients with type 2 diabetes, established CVD or additional CV risk factors
trialDesign.duration: Mean follow-up 3.5 years (intensive glycemic regimen stopped early)
trialDesign.intervention: Intensive glucose lowering targeting HbA1c <6.0% via study-supervised formulary
trialDesign.primaryEndpoint: First nonfatal MI, nonfatal stroke, or death from cardiovascular causes
trialDesign.overview: Intensive glucose lowering did not reduce the primary composite (HR 0.90; P=0.16) but increased all-cause mortality, leading to early discontinuation of the intensive regimen.

headings.background: Epidemiologic data link higher HbA1c to cardiovascular events; ACCORD tested whether normalizing HbA1c would reduce events in high-risk type 2 diabetes.
headings.design: 10,251 patients randomized to intensive (<6.0%) vs standard (7.0–7.9%) glycemic targets; factorial BP and lipid trials in subsets.
headings.population: Mean age 62; 38% women; 35% prior cardiovascular event; high CV risk type 2 diabetes.
headings.intervention: Intensive individualized formulary with monthly visits initially; mean 4.4 regimen changes/year.
headings.comparator: Standard glycemic management with visits every 4 months; target HbA1c 7.0–7.9%.
headings.followUp: Mean 3.5 years; intensive glycemic strategy stopped 17 months early for safety.

outcomes.label: All-cause mortality
outcomes.interpretation: 1.0% absolute increase; ~1 death per 95 patients treated

safetyHighlights: Hypoglycemia requiring assistance: 3.1%/year intensive vs 1.0% standard
Weight gain >10 kg: 27.8% intensive vs 14.1% standard; fluid retention more frequent
trialSummary.clinicalBottomLine: In high-risk type 2 diabetes, intensive glucose lowering to near-normal HbA1c does not reduce major cardiovascular events and increases mortality — avoid overly aggressive targets in this population.

inclusionCriteria: Type 2 diabetes with HbA1c ≥7.5% despite treatment or willingness to defer pharmacotherapy
Age 40–79 with established CVD, or age 55–79 with subclinical CVD or ≥2 CV risk factors
exclusionCriteria: Frequent severe hypoglycemia or unwillingness to inject insulin
keyTakeaways: Primary composite neutral (HR 0.90; P=0.16); nonfatal MI significantly reduced.
All-cause mortality ↑22% with intensive therapy — regimen stopped early at 3.5 years.
Harm signal: ~1 extra death per 95 patients over 3.5 years; more hypoglycemia and weight gain.

items.title: ACCORD — Main Points (High-Yield Overview)
items.content:
Background and Hypothesis: Epidemiologic studies established a direct relationship between glycated hemoglobin and cardiovascular events in type 2 diabetes. ACCORD tested whether targeting normal HbA1c (<6.0%) would reduce cardiovascular events in middle-aged and older patients with type 2 diabetes and established CVD or additional risk factors.

Study Design: 10,251 patients randomized to intensive therapy (HbA1c <6.0%) vs standard therapy (7.0–7.9%). Double 2×2 factorial design with concurrent BP and lipid substudies.

Glycemic Achievements: By 1 year, median HbA1c stabilized at 6.4% (intensive) vs 7.5% (standard).

Primary Outcomes: Intensive therapy did not significantly reduce the primary composite of nonfatal MI, nonfatal stroke, or CV death (HR 0.90; P=0.16), though nonfatal MI was reduced.

Mortality and Safety: Intensive regimen discontinued after mean 3.5 years due to higher all-cause mortality (257 vs 203 deaths; ~1 extra death per 95 patients). Hypoglycemia requiring assistance and >10 kg weight gain were more frequent with intensive therapy.

Conclusion: Targeting normal HbA1c in this high-risk population increased mortality by 1.0% over 3.5 years — a previously unrecognized harm of intensive glucose lowering.
keyPoints: Primary composite neutral (HR 0.90; P=0.16) · nonfatal MI ↓
All-cause mortality ↑22% — intensive regimen stopped early
NNT harm ~95 patients for 1 extra death over 3.5 years
items.reference: The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.

items.question: Why was the ACCORD trial specifically designed to target a glycated hemoglobin level below 6.0%?
items.answer: The trial was designed to test whether targeting normal glycated hemoglobin levels would reduce the rate of cardiovascular events, a hypothesis generated from epidemiologic studies showing a graded relationship between higher glycated hemoglobin and increased cardiovascular risk.
keyPoints: The trial was designed to test whether targeting normal glycated hemoglobin levels would reduce the rate of cardiovascular events, a hypothesis generated from epidemiologic studies showing a graded relationship between higher glycated hemoglobin and increased cardiovascular risk.
items.reference: ACCORD Q1: The trial was designed to test whether targeting normal glycated hemoglobin leve... — The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.

items.question: How did the independent data and safety monitoring committee respond to the interim mortality trends in January 2008?
items.answer: After reviewing mortality trends, the committee concluded that the harm from an increased rate of death in the intensive-therapy group outweighed potential benefits and recommended discontinuing the intensive regimen for safety reasons.
keyPoints: After reviewing mortality trends, the committee concluded that the harm from an increased rate of death in the intensive-therapy group outweighed potential benefits and recommended discontinuing the intensive regimen for safety reasons.
items.reference: ACCORD Q2: After reviewing mortality trends, the committee concluded that the harm from an ... — The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.

items.question: Why did the primary composite outcome fail to achieve statistical significance despite a lower event count in the intensive group?
items.answer: Although there were fewer primary outcomes in the intensive-therapy group (352 events) compared to the standard-therapy group (371 events), the hazard ratio of 0.90 yielded a P value of 0.16, which did not meet the threshold for statistical significance.
keyPoints: Although there were fewer primary outcomes in the intensive-therapy group (352 events) compared to the standard-therapy group (371 events), the hazard ratio of 0.90 yielded a P value of 0.16, which did not meet the threshold for statistical significance.
items.reference: ACCORD Q3: Although there were fewer primary outcomes in the intensive-therapy group (352 e... — The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.

items.question: How did the intensive therapy specifically alter the incidence of nonfatal myocardial infarctions?
items.answer: The intensive-therapy group had a significantly lower rate of nonfatal myocardial infarction compared to the standard-therapy group, occurring at a rate of 3.6% versus 4.6% (P=0.004).
keyPoints: The intensive-therapy group had a significantly lower rate of nonfatal myocardial infarction compared to the standard-therapy group, occurring at a rate of 3.6% versus 4.6% (P=0.004).
items.reference: ACCORD Q4: The intensive-therapy group had a significantly lower rate of nonfatal myocardia... — The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.

items.question: Why do the authors suggest that if cardiovascular benefits exist from intensive therapy, they might require a long time to manifest?
items.answer: The rates of the primary outcome only began to separate between the two study groups after 3 years, suggesting that any potential benefit from intensive glucose lowering may take several years to emerge, during which time mortality risk is elevated.
keyPoints: The rates of the primary outcome only began to separate between the two study groups after 3 years, suggesting that any potential benefit from intensive glucose lowering may take several years to emerge, during which time mortality risk is elevated.
items.reference: ACCORD Q5: The rates of the primary outcome only began to separate between the two study gr... — The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.

items.question: How did researchers practically achieve the rapid reduction in glycated hemoglobin for the intensive-therapy group?
items.answer: Researchers individualized therapeutic regimens using a study-supervised formulary, requiring patients in the intensive group to attend monthly visits initially and modifying their drug regimen a mean of 4.4 times per year.
keyPoints: Researchers individualized therapeutic regimens using a study-supervised formulary, requiring patients in the intensive group to attend monthly visits initially and modifying their drug regimen a mean of 4.4 times per year.
items.reference: ACCORD Q6: Researchers individualized therapeutic regimens using a study-supervised formula... — The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.

items.question: Why was the increase in mortality labeled a "previously unrecognized harm"?
items.answer: Prior clinical trials, such as the UK Prospective Diabetes Study and the Veterans Affairs Diabetes Feasibility Trial, had not clearly established or identified a significant increase in mortality associated with intensive glucose lowering.
keyPoints: Prior clinical trials, such as the UK Prospective Diabetes Study and the Veterans Affairs Diabetes Feasibility Trial, had not clearly established or identified a significant increase in mortality associated with intensive glucose lowering.
items.reference: ACCORD Q7: Prior clinical trials, such as the UK Prospective Diabetes Study and the Veteran... — The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.

items.question: How did the trial manage patients whose baseline characteristics included concurrent blood pressure or lipid abnormalities?
items.answer: The trial utilized a double two-by-two factorial design, whereby a subset of 4,733 patients were randomly assigned to intensive or standard blood pressure therapy, and another 5,518 patients were randomly assigned to receive fenofibrate or placebo for lipid control.
keyPoints: The trial utilized a double two-by-two factorial design, whereby a subset of 4,733 patients were randomly assigned to intensive or standard blood pressure therapy, and another 5,518 patients were randomly assigned to receive fenofibrate or placebo for lipid control.
items.reference: ACCORD Q8: The trial utilized a double two-by-two factorial design, whereby a subset of 4,7... — The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.

items.question: Why was an intention-to-treat approach used for the primary statistical analyses?
items.answer: An intention-to-treat analysis ensures that patient outcomes are compared based strictly on their original randomized study-group assignments, preserving the structural integrity of the randomized trial.
keyPoints: An intention-to-treat analysis ensures that patient outcomes are compared based strictly on their original randomized study-group assignments, preserving the structural integrity of the randomized trial.
items.reference: ACCORD Q9: An intention-to-treat analysis ensures that patient outcomes are compared based ... — The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.

items.question: How did the incidence of severe weight gain contrast between the two study cohorts?
items.answer: Weight gain of more than 10 kg from baseline occurred in 27.8% of patients in the intensive-therapy group, which was nearly double the 14.1% rate observed in the standard-therapy group.
keyPoints: Weight gain of more than 10 kg from baseline occurred in 27.8% of patients in the intensive-therapy group, which was nearly double the 14.1% rate observed in the standard-therapy group.
items.reference: ACCORD Q10: Weight gain of more than 10 kg from baseline occurred in 27.8% of patients in th... — The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.

items.question: Why did the prespecified subgroup analyses raise the possibility that certain patients might benefit from intensive therapy?
items.answer: Analyses showed evidence of heterogeneity, suggesting that intensive therapy patients with a baseline glycated hemoglobin of 8.0% or less, or those without a prior cardiovascular event, may have experienced fewer cardiovascular events than their standard-therapy counterparts.
keyPoints: Analyses showed evidence of heterogeneity, suggesting that intensive therapy patients with a baseline glycated hemoglobin of 8.0% or less, or those without a prior cardiovascular event, may have experienced fewer cardiovascular events than their standard-therapy counterparts.
items.reference: ACCORD Q11: Analyses showed evidence of heterogeneity, suggesting that intensive therapy pat... — The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.

items.question: How did the study confirm the accuracy and objectivity of key outcomes like myocardial infarction and stroke?
items.answer: Key outcomes were rigorously adjudicated by a central committee whose members were kept unaware of the patients' study-group assignments, using predefined criteria.
keyPoints: Key outcomes were rigorously adjudicated by a central committee whose members were kept unaware of the patients' study-group assignments, using predefined criteria.
items.reference: ACCORD Q12: Key outcomes were rigorously adjudicated by a central committee whose members we... — The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.

items.question: Why are the post hoc analyses regarding the cause of increased mortality considered only "exploratory"?
items.answer: Because the study was not designed to test the individual components of the intervention strategy, and glucose-lowering therapies were adjusted post-randomization based on clinical response, these analyses cannot definitively identify or exclude a single specific cause for the mortality.
keyPoints: Because the study was not designed to test the individual components of the intervention strategy, and glucose-lowering therapies were adjusted post-randomization based on clinical response, these analyses cannot definitively identify or exclude a single specific cause for the mortality.
items.reference: ACCORD Q13: Because the study was not designed to test the individual components of the inte... — The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.

items.question: How was the primary composite outcome explicitly defined in this study?
items.answer: The primary outcome was defined as the first occurrence of a nonfatal myocardial infarction, a nonfatal stroke, or death from cardiovascular causes.
keyPoints: The primary outcome was defined as the first occurrence of a nonfatal myocardial infarction, a nonfatal stroke, or death from cardiovascular causes.
items.reference: ACCORD Q14: The primary outcome was defined as the first occurrence of a nonfatal myocardial... — The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.

items.question: Why did the intensive-therapy group utilize more drugs from every medication class compared to the standard group?
items.answer: Achieving the aggressive target of a glycated hemoglobin level below 6.0% required a customized, comprehensive approach that inherently resulted in a greater exposure to single drugs, drug combinations, and frequent dosing changes.
keyPoints: Achieving the aggressive target of a glycated hemoglobin level below 6.0% required a customized, comprehensive approach that inherently resulted in a greater exposure to single drugs, drug combinations, and frequent dosing changes.
items.reference: ACCORD Q15: Achieving the aggressive target of a glycated hemoglobin level below 6.0% requir... — The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.

items.question: What was the median glycated hemoglobin level achieved and stabilized by the intensive-therapy group at 1 year?
items.explanation: Median HbA1c stabilized at 6.4% in the intensive group at 1 year.
items.reference: ACCORD MCQ 1: Median HbA1c stabilized at 6.4% in the intensive group at 1 year. — The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.

items.question: How did the annualized rate of hypoglycemic episodes requiring medical assistance compare between the groups?
items.explanation: Hypoglycemia requiring assistance: 3.1%/year intensive vs 1.0% standard.
items.reference: ACCORD MCQ 2: Hypoglycemia requiring assistance: 3.1%/year intensive vs 1.0% standard. — The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.

items.question: What was the primary reason the intensive glucose-lowering regimen was prematurely discontinued?
options: A lack of funding to continue the monthly patient visits
A significantly higher rate of all-cause mortality in the intensive group
The intensive group achieved its cardiovascular benefit threshold early
High rates of severe liver toxicity
items.explanation: Intensive regimen stopped due to significantly higher all-cause mortality.
items.reference: ACCORD MCQ 3: Intensive regimen stopped due to significantly higher all-cause mortality. — The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.

items.question: The absolute increase of 1.0% in mortality during the follow-up period translates to one extra death for every how many patients treated with intensive therapy?
items.explanation: 1.0% absolute mortality increase ≈ 1 extra death per 95 patients treated.
items.reference: ACCORD MCQ 4: 1.0% absolute mortality increase ≈ 1 extra death per 95 patients treated. — The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.

items.question: In which prespecified subgroup did interaction tests suggest the intensive therapy might lead to fewer cardiovascular events?
options: Patients with a baseline glycated hemoglobin level over 8.0%
Patients with no previous cardiovascular event before randomization
items.explanation: Heterogeneity suggested possible benefit in patients without prior cardiovascular event.
items.reference: ACCORD MCQ 5: Heterogeneity suggested possible benefit in patients without prior cardiovascular event. — The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.

items.question: How did the rate of death specifically from cardiovascular causes differ between the intensive and standard therapy groups?
items.explanation: CV death: 2.6% intensive vs 1.8% standard (HR 1.35).
items.reference: ACCORD MCQ 6: CV death: 2.6% intensive vs 1.8% standard (HR 1.35). — The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.

items.question: Which of the following was a key exclusion criterion for patients attempting to enroll in the ACCORD trial?
options: A baseline glycated hemoglobin level of 7.5%
A body-mass index of more than 45
items.explanation: BMI >45 was an exclusion criterion.
items.reference: ACCORD MCQ 7: BMI >45 was an exclusion criterion. — The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.

items.question: Statistically, the study was originally designed to have 89% power to detect what percentage reduction in the rate of the primary outcome?
items.explanation: Study powered (89%) to detect a 15% reduction in primary outcome.
items.reference: ACCORD MCQ 8: Study powered (89%) to detect a 15% reduction in primary outcome. — The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.

items.question: Which adverse event occurred significantly more frequently in the intensive-therapy group?
options: Motor vehicle accidents in which the patient was the driver
Fatal or nonfatal congestive heart failure
items.explanation: Fluid retention was significantly more frequent with intensive therapy.
items.reference: ACCORD MCQ 9: Fluid retention was significantly more frequent with intensive therapy. — The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.

items.question: How did the rate of nonfatal stroke differ between the intensive and standard groups?
options: It was significantly higher in the intensive group (2.6% vs 1.8%)
It was significantly lower in the intensive group (1.2% vs 2.4%)
There was no significant difference (1.3% vs 1.2%)
It was the primary driver of the mortality difference
items.explanation: Nonfatal stroke rates did not differ significantly (1.3% vs 1.2%).
items.reference: ACCORD MCQ 10: Nonfatal stroke rates did not differ significantly (1.3% vs 1.2%). — The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.

items.question: Which nonglycemic medication was received by significantly fewer patients in the intensive-therapy group compared to the standard-therapy group?
items.explanation: Fewer intensive-therapy patients received ACE inhibitors.
items.reference: ACCORD MCQ 11: Fewer intensive-therapy patients received ACE inhibitors. — The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.

items.question: What was the mean duration of follow-up for patients at the time the data and safety monitoring committee recommended discontinuing the intensive regimen?
items.explanation: Mean follow-up was 3.5 years at time of intensive-regimen discontinuation.
items.reference: ACCORD MCQ 12: Mean follow-up was 3.5 years at time of intensive-regimen discontinuation. — The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.

items.question: How frequently were patients in the standard-therapy group scheduled for glycemic-management visits?
items.explanation: Standard group had glycemic visits every 4 months.
items.reference: ACCORD MCQ 13: Standard group had glycemic visits every 4 months. — The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.

items.question: Approximately what percentage of the randomized cohort in the ACCORD trial was female?
items.explanation: 38% of participants were women.
items.reference: ACCORD MCQ 14: 38% of participants were women. — The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.

items.question: How much did the median glycated hemoglobin level fall in the intensive-therapy group within the first 4 months after randomization?
items.explanation: Median HbA1c fell from 8.1% to 6.7% within 4 months.
items.reference: ACCORD MCQ 15: Median HbA1c fell from 8.1% to 6.7% within 4 months. — The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.

items.question: Which component of the primary composite outcome saw a statistically significant reduction in the intensive-therapy group?
items.explanation: Nonfatal MI was significantly reduced (3.6% vs 4.6%; P=0.004).
items.reference: ACCORD MCQ 16: Nonfatal MI was significantly reduced (3.6% vs 4.6%; P=0.004). — The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.

items.question: What was the target glycated hemoglobin level for the standard-therapy group?
items.explanation: Standard therapy targeted HbA1c 7.0–7.9%.
items.reference: ACCORD MCQ 17: Standard therapy targeted HbA1c 7.0–7.9%. — The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.

items.question: How was exposure to glucose-lowering drugs statistically summarized for the two study groups?
options: By absolute milligram dosage per day
By the number of patients receiving a prescription and total person-years of prescriptions
By the ratio of oral medications to insulin injections
By the overall financial cost of the formulary
items.explanation: Drug exposure summarized by prescription counts and person-years.
items.reference: ACCORD MCQ 18: Drug exposure summarized by prescription counts and person-years. — The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.

items.question: What was the relative increase in total mortality for the intensive-therapy group compared to the standard-therapy group?
items.explanation: Total mortality was 22% higher in the intensive group (257 vs 203 deaths).
items.reference: ACCORD MCQ 19: Total mortality was 22% higher in the intensive group (257 vs 203 deaths). — The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.

items.question: What was the mean weight gain at 3 years for patients in the intensive-therapy group?
items.explanation: Mean weight gain at 3 years was 3.5 kg in the intensive group.
items.reference: ACCORD MCQ 20: Mean weight gain at 3 years was 3.5 kg in the intensive group. — The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.

items.statement: The trial successfully proved that targeting normal glycated hemoglobin levels below 6.0% significantly reduces major cardiovascular events.
items.explanation: Primary composite was not significantly reduced (HR 0.90; P=0.16).
items.reference: ACCORD T/F 1: Primary composite was not significantly reduced (HR 0.90; P=0.16). — The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.

items.statement: The intensive-therapy group experienced a significantly higher rate of nonfatal myocardial infarctions than the standard-therapy group.
items.explanation: Nonfatal MI was significantly lower in the intensive group (3.6% vs 4.6%).
items.reference: ACCORD T/F 2: Nonfatal MI was significantly lower in the intensive group (3.6% vs 4.6%). — The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.

items.statement: The independent data and safety monitoring committee recommended terminating the intensive regimen 17 months before the scheduled end of the study.
items.explanation: Intensive regimen stopped 17 months early after DSMB review.
items.reference: ACCORD T/F 3: Intensive regimen stopped 17 months early after DSMB review. — The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.

items.statement: Patients in the intensive-therapy group required modifications to their glucose-lowering regimens a mean of 4.4 times per year.
items.explanation: Mean 4.4 regimen modifications per year in intensive group.
items.reference: ACCORD T/F 4: Mean 4.4 regimen modifications per year in intensive group. — The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.

items.statement: The study found clear, definitive evidence that a specific interaction between drug classes was the direct cause of the increased mortality in the intensive-therapy group.
items.explanation: No definitive drug-class interaction identified; mortality analyses were exploratory.
items.reference: ACCORD T/F 5: No definitive drug-class interaction identified; mortality analyses were exploratory. — The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.

items.statement: A serum creatinine level of more than 1.5 mg per deciliter was a key exclusion criterion for the trial.
items.explanation: Serum creatinine >1.5 mg/dL was an exclusion criterion.
items.reference: ACCORD T/F 6: Serum creatinine >1.5 mg/dL was an exclusion criterion. — The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.

items.statement: The intensive glucose-lowering therapy significantly reduced the rate of nonfatal stroke.
items.explanation: Nonfatal stroke was not significantly reduced.
items.reference: ACCORD T/F 7: Nonfatal stroke was not significantly reduced. — The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.

items.statement: The trial utilized a factorial design to concurrently evaluate blood-pressure and lipid interventions in subsets of the patients.
items.explanation: Double 2×2 factorial design for glycemia, BP, and lipid substudies.
items.reference: ACCORD T/F 8: Double 2×2 factorial design for glycemia, BP, and lipid substudies. — The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.

items.statement: The overall mortality rate for patients in both study groups was lower than what has been reported in epidemiologic studies of similar patients.
items.explanation: Observed mortality was lower than epidemiologic expectations.
items.reference: ACCORD T/F 9: Observed mortality was lower than epidemiologic expectations. — The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.

items.statement: The intensive-therapy group achieved a stable median glycated hemoglobin level of 6.4% at the 1-year mark.
items.explanation: Median HbA1c 6.4% at 1 year in intensive group.
items.reference: ACCORD T/F 10: Median HbA1c 6.4% at 1 year in intensive group. — The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.

items.statement: The intensive-therapy group had a statistically significant increase in the incidence of fluid retention compared to the standard-therapy group.
items.explanation: Fluid retention was significantly more frequent with intensive therapy.
items.reference: ACCORD T/F 11: Fluid retention was significantly more frequent with intensive therapy. — The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.

items.statement: By the end of the intervention period, over 97% of patients had their vital status ascertained within the previous 12 months.
items.explanation: >97% had vital status confirmed within prior 12 months.
items.reference: ACCORD T/F 12: >97% had vital status confirmed within prior 12 months. — The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.

items.statement: The hazard ratio for death from cardiovascular causes was 1.35, indicating a significantly higher risk in the intensive-therapy group.
items.explanation: CV death HR 1.35 in intensive vs standard therapy.
items.reference: ACCORD T/F 13: CV death HR 1.35 in intensive vs standard therapy. — The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.

items.statement: Prior epidemiologic studies had shown an inverse relationship where higher glycated hemoglobin levels were associated with a decreased risk of cardiovascular events.
items.explanation: Epidemiology showed higher HbA1c associated with increased CV risk, not decreased.
items.reference: ACCORD T/F 14: Epidemiology showed higher HbA1c associated with increased CV risk, not decreased. — The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.

items.statement: Hypoglycemia requiring any assistance occurred in 16.2% of the intensive-therapy group, compared to only 5.1% of the standard-therapy group.
items.explanation: Any-assistance hypoglycemia: 16.2% intensive vs 5.1% standard.
items.reference: ACCORD T/F 15: Any-assistance hypoglycemia: 16.2% intensive vs 5.1% standard. — The ACCORD Study Group. Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;358:2545-2559.
